coredata.dc:title: Monodispersed magnetographene quantum dot nanocomposites for delivery of silibinin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Takke, Anjali
Shende, Pravin
coredata.dc:description: Silibinin shows multi-applications and therapeutic activities such as anticancer, antioxidant, hypocholesterolemic, cardioprotective, neuroprotective and hepatoprotective but exhibits limited clinical applications due to poor oral bioavailability and in-vitro in-vivo biological correlation. The present study focused on the characterization of magnetic graphene quantum dot nanocomposites for silibinin delivery with a specific interest in kidney cancer treatment optimized by a central composite design. The effect of the composition of polylactic-co-glycolic acid (PLGA), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and silibinin on the mean particle size and encapsulation efficiency was studied. The properties of the synthesized magnetic graphene nanocomposites by using silibinin magnetic nanoparticles determined by central composite design method revealed particle size below 300 nm, high encapsulation efficiency (81.27 %) and cumulative in-vitro drug release exhibited in sustained manner. In vitro cellular uptake studies with kidney cancer cell line (A-498) demonstrated that the magnetic graphene quantum dot nanocomposites presented higher cellular uptake of silibinin and cytotoxicity than graphene quantum dots. The in-vivo pharmacokinetic study demonstrated a significant improvement in drug bioavailability with better mean residence time due to prolong exposure in systemic circulation. Therefore, monodispersed magnetic graphene quantum dot nanocomposites are considered as an interesting alternative for silibinin delivery against kidney cancer cell line A-498.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Silibinin
Central composite design
Cytotoxicity
A-498 cancer cell lines

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental approach of silibinin-loaded magnetic nanoparticles
Synthesis of SIN-mag-NPs using CCD design

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Contour plots illustrating the effect of independent variables on (a) PS (b) EE.
TEM images of: a) GQDs and b) SIN-MGNs.
ATR-FTIR spectra of: a) drug b) SIN-mag-NPs c) GQDs and d) SIN-MGNs.
Magnetization curves of: A) SIN-mag-NPs and B) SIN-MGNs.
Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) GQDs and (b) SIN-MGNs.
Release behaviour of SIN from SIN-MGNs.
% Inhibition of GQDs and SIN-MGNs in different concentrations against A-498 cells.
Cellular uptake of A498 cells after 4 h treatment. Cell lysate Ex/Em 365/460 nm.
The SIN concentration-time profile after administration in mice plasma a single i.v dose of 50 mg/kg (n = 3).
The selected experimental parameters used in CCD.
CCD table include various trials, PS, and EE.
Analysis of variance (ANOVA) test for each experimental response.
Optimized formulation conditions offered by software.
Pharmacokinetic parameters of SIN and SIN-MGNs i.v. injected in mice at the equivalent 50 mg/kg dose.

head.ce:title: Monodispersed magnetographene quantum dot nanocomposites for delivery of silibinin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shobhaben Pratapbhai Patel School of Pharmacy and Technology Management, SVKM’s NMIMS, V. L. Mehta Road, Vile Parle (W), Mumbai, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shobhaben Pratapbhai Patel School of Pharmacy and Technology Management
sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: SVKM’s NMIMS, V. L. Mehta Road, Vile Parle (W)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shobhaben Pratapbhai Patel School of Pharmacy and Technology Management,SVKM’s NMIMS, V. L. Mehta Road, Vile Parle (W), Mumbai, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silibinin shows multi-applications and therapeutic activities such as anticancer, antioxidant, hypocholesterolemic, cardioprotective, neuroprotective and hepatoprotective but exhibits limited clinical applications due to poor oral bioavailability and in-vitro in-vivo biological correlation. The present study focused on the characterization of magnetic graphene quantum dot nanocomposites for silibinin delivery with a specific interest in kidney cancer treatment optimized by a central composite design. The effect of the composition of polylactic-co-glycolic acid (PLGA), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and silibinin on the mean particle size and encapsulation efficiency was studied. The properties of the synthesized magnetic graphene nanocomposites by using silibinin magnetic nanoparticles determined by central composite design method revealed particle size below 300 nm, high encapsulation efficiency (81.27 %) and cumulative in-vitro drug release exhibited in sustained manner. In vitro cellular uptake studies with kidney cancer cell line (A-498) demonstrated that the magnetic graphene quantum dot nanocomposites presented higher cellular uptake of silibinin and cytotoxicity than graphene quantum dots. The in-vivo pharmacokinetic study demonstrated a significant improvement in drug bioavailability with better mean residence time due to prolong exposure in systemic circulation. Therefore, monodispersed magnetic graphene quantum dot nanocomposites are considered as an interesting alternative for silibinin delivery against kidney cancer cell line A-498.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancers of the kidney and renal pelvis have rapidly become more common in the developed world over the past decades [1,2]. Chemotherapy is the main type of cancer therapeutics, but its efficiency restricted by the development of drug resistance [3,4]. Thus, the rapid advancement of nanotechnology contributes for newer therapeutic strategies for the management of kidney cancer [5,6]. As silymarin main component is silibinin (SIN) which is sparingly soluble in water which thus the clinical applications and therapeutic efficacy of SIN are constrained [7–11]. The antitumor potential of SIN is evident against various cancers such as lung, stomach, prostate, colon, bladder, skin, pancreas and kidney in the last two decades [12–14]. The low solubility of SIN significantly reduces its bioavailability and makes impossible to use it in the form of injectable dosage forms. The different nanoformulation approaches like polymeric nanoparticles, micelles, carbon nanotubes, emulsomes, niosomes, magnetic nanoparticles, thermoresponsive hydrogels and phospholipid complexes enhance the bioavailability of SIN for improving targeted and cytotoxic actions in cancer cells [9,11,15–21].

Graphene quantum dot (GQD) is an emerging class of nanocarbon material, exhibit photoluminescence property, excellent photostability and great biocompatibility with promising applications such as fluorescence imaging, biosensing and drug delivery [22–24]. GQDs possess lower cytotoxicity, large surface area and potential for surface functionalization that bring about as more effective drug loading carrier [25–27]. Furthermore, the recent developments are carried on the combination of GQDs with metallic or polymeric nanomaterials for imaging and drug delivery aspects [26,28,29]. Magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) especially iron oxide and its nanocomposite with GQDs were applied for enhancing optical limiting behavior, MRI contrast (magnetic resonance imaging) and drug delivery [30–33]. Loading of GQDs onto MNPs are preferred over other nanomaterials as they act as excellent tumor diagnostic devices, treatment alternatives and multi-therapeutic tools for magnetic hyperthermia and photothermal therapy [34–36]. Polymeric nanoparticles have been tested to deliver wide range of drugs, such as small hydrophilic and hydrophobic drugs, vaccines, peptides, and biological macromolecules, via several routes of administration [37–40]. Polylactic-co-glycolic acid (PLGA) is a biodegradable polymer extensively used in the formation of nanoparticulate carriers [41–43]. The delivery of actives with PLGA nanoparticles (PNPs) offers certain unique advantages like rapid drug release, lesser drug degradation, enhancement of drug bioavailability, higher rate of hydrolysis and lower toxicity [44–46]. PNPs have a potential for influencing the mechanism of drug release and improving the bioavailability of poorly soluble drugs like curcumin, zoledronic acid, gentamicin sulfate, haloperidol, paclitaxel, vancomycin, estradiol, sparfloxacin etc. [47–53]. The delivery of the drug might be site specific as the combination of MNPs and GQDs could be operated by magnetic fields. The present study was to develop magnetographene quantum dot nanocomposites (MGNs) with PNPs for delivery of SIN as fluorescent materials and assessed for particle size (PS), morphology, zeta potential (ZP), encapsulation efficiency (EE), magnetic property, fluorescence and drug releasing property. Also, cytotoxic activity, cellular uptake study and in-vivo pharmacokinetic studies were also carried out.

Graphene quantum dots were obtained from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India. SIN was obtained from Sigma Aldrich, Mumbai, India. PLGA (50:50, Resomer RG 502H) was obtained from Boehringer Ingelheim (Germany). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO), USA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental approach of silibinin-loaded magnetic nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Solvent evaporation method was used to prepare silibinin-loaded magnetic nanoparticles (SIN-mag-NPs) with slight modifications in previously reported technique [44,45]. SIN and PLGA were separately dissolved in organic solvents. 10 mg of MNPs and dissolved SIN were added in PLGA solution and homogenized at 8000 rpm for 3 min. This organic phase was added dropwise to a PVA solution, which was stirred at a rate of 8000 rpm using homogenizer for 5 min. The resulting emulsion was probe sonicated (SM-120PS, India) for 3 min. Then organic solvent was evaporated using a rotary evaporator under reduced pressure and NPs were purified by centrifugation (10,000 rpm for 15 min, Thermo Sorvall Ultracentrifuge, MTX, USA). Two formulation criterions were optimized for their effects on the PS (Y1) and EE (Y2) of the developed NPs applying Design-Expert® Version 12 software. Based on formulation trials, PLGA (mg) (×1), PVA (%) (×2), and SIN content (mg) (×3) were considered as important variables affecting the preparation of NPs. Two parameters (PS and % EE) were examined at five different levels (-1.782, -1, 0, 1, 1.782) using a CCD ( Table 1).

First 20 mg of optimised SIN-mag-NPs were incorporated into the solution of 3.2 mg N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 5 mg carbodiimide (EDC) in 10 mL distilled water. Further, 4 mL GQDs solution (2 mg/mL) was added into above solution and stirred at room temperature for 5 h. Finally, the obtained MGNs were collected by magnet, washed by acetone and dried [27,54].

Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments, UK) was used to determine the PS, polydispersity index (PDI) and ZP of the different formulations of SIN-mag-NPs and SIN-MGNs. The % EE was calculated by following equation.
% EE = [(CT ̶ CS/CT)] X 100
CT is the initial amount of SIN and CS is the amount of SIN remained in the supernatant.

ATR-FTIR spectroscopy measurements of SIN, GQDs and SIN-MGNs and SIN-mag-NPs were obtained by ATR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, USA). Morphology and structure of GQDs and SIN-MGNs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-Z300FSC, JEOL Ltd. Japan). The magnetic property of SIN-MGNs and SIN-mag-NPs was identified by vibrating sample magnetometer (SES, VSM-1000, India). X-ray fluorescence spectra (XRF, ARL-9800, USA) of GQDs and SIN-MGNs were obtained by exciting the samples at 350 nm.

In vitro release profile (IVR) of SIN from MGNs was achieved using a dialysis membrane molecular weight cut-off 12–14 kDa) (Spectra por, Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA) with 30 mL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) at a stirring rate 100 rpm and temperature 37 ± 0.5 °C. At allocated time intervals, 3 mL of dialysis solution was withdrawn, this volume was replaced by fresh dissolution media and further analyzed on UV-Vis spectrophotometer at 288 nm for SIN.

A-498 cells were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity test of formulations GQDs and SIN-MGNs. 100 µL of A-498 cells were seeded in 96-well plates and treated with different concentrations (0.001–10 µg/mL) of the test formulations and incubated for 48 h. MTT reagent was added to the wells and incubated for 4 h, thereafter 50 mL of DMSO was added to each well to dissolve the formazan crystals and read at 550 nm on a plate reader. Results were represented as % growth inhibition compared to controls and 50% inhibition (IC50) was also determined.

A-498 cells were allowed to reach to confluence in a six well plate. The cells were treated with 100 ug/mL of test samples for 4 h. Post treatment plates along with cells were washed with 2 mL PBS 4 °C thrice. The cells were lysed with a cell lysis buffer. The lysate was centrifuged at 10,000 RPM for 20 min and analyzed for fluorescence on a plate reader (BMG Fluostar) Ex/Em 365/460.

The Institutional Animal Ethics Committee (IAEC) approved the protocol with an approval number of CPCSEA/IAEC/PC15/2019. The animals (adult Swiss Albino mice weighing 22–25 g) were distributed into two groups, each contain 12 mice. The route of administration for S-PNPs and SIN (50 mg/kg) was the intravenous route. At 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 10, 24 and 48 h post-dosing, approximately 0.5 mL blood samples were removed from the retro-orbital plexus into the heparinized centrifuge tubes. Plasma was prepared by centrifugation (8000 rpm × 10 min) at 4 °C and stored at -80 °C until analysis. The samples were analyzed through LCMS/MS (Shimadzu, LCMS-8040) following the procedure described by Pawar et al. [55]. PK solver software was to determine pharmacokinetic parameters such as Cmax, area under the concentration-time curve from dosing time 0 to time t (AUC0-t), area under the concentration-time curve from time 0 extrapolated to infinite time (AUC0-∞), and MRT. Relative bioavailability (RB) for SIN-MGNs against SIN drug solution was calculated as follows:
RB = AUC SIN-PLGA nanoparticles/AUC SIN drug solution

The formulation SIN-MGNs was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C with a testing regularity of 0 day, 1 month, 2 months and 3 months. The formulation showing optimal PS, ZP, % EE and IVR of SIN in NPs was considered further for stability studies.
The data were presented as the mean ± SD and significant differences were calculated using one-way ANOVA. Values of p < 0.05 were considered significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of SIN-mag-NPs using CCD design
ce:section.ce:para: CCD was used as a tool to the effect of the independent variables (×1–×3) on the dependent variables (Y1–Y2). Table 1 represents the selected experimental parameters used in CCD and results of various trials with respect to PS and EE are presented in Table 2.

Contour plots showed significant impact of PLGA and PVA on the PS of NPs ( Fig. 1). The mean PS of various trials is shown in Table 2. The polynomial equations for linear mathematical model (Eqs. 1 and 2) denoted the quantitative effect of the process variables (×1, ×2 and ×3) and their interaction on the selected responses. The results of statistical analysis are represented in Table 3. The final optimised SIN-mag-NP formulation acquired from the analysis provided by CCD design model ( Table 4).

The polymer concentration was increased from 13.83 mg to 100 mg and its effect on PS and EE (%) was evaluated. The present study indicated that the NPs possessed PS (Y1) values ranging between 152.05 ± 0.19 and 307.24 ± 0.91 nm (Table 2) and a narrow range of variability in PDI (0.104–0.629). Based on the results, it is estimated that the concentration of polymer is directly proportional to PS of NPs. Hence, the viscosity of the organic phase was enhanced which caused increase in the viscous forces withstanding droplet breakdown therefore, larger NPs were prepared, leading to increase in PS [47]. The same observation was detected with an increase amount of PVA, but increase in PS was not as prominent as mentioned in Table 2.

PVA might be oriented at the interface between the organic solution and aqueous medium for better stabilization of the system and helped in the formation of small size particles [49,56]. Out of 3 factors studied, the amount of PLGA and concentration of PVA were found to possess significant effect on the PS. The R2 value for the model was found to be 0.9323 as shown in Table 3. This actual R2 value was greater than the adjusted R2 value (0.9633).

In this study, EE (Y2) of NPs was obtained in the range of 62–92% (Table 2). The R2 value for the model was found to be 0.8938 (Table 3). This actual R2 value was greater than the adjusted R2 value (0.9176) and all selected parameters were significant at p ≤ 0.05. As illustrated in Fig. 1, by increasing the concentration of PLGA, viscosity of organic phase increases which interferes the drug’s diffusion mechanism from the organic into aqueous phase with enhancement in % EE [44,57,58].

Optimised formulation of SIN-mag-NPs was selected for the preparation of SIN-MGNs (Table 4). PS, ZP and % EE of the developed SIN-MGNs are shown in Table 5. This formulation was used for the development of MGNs and further characterized by PS, PDI, ZP, % EE, ATR-FTIR spectroscopy, VSM, IVR, cytotoxicity test and in-vivo pharmacokinetic study.
The TEM images of the GQDs and SIN-MGNs are shown in Fig. 2. It represented spherical shape of NPs with good dispersibility [25,59].

ATR-FTIR spectrum for SIN-mag-NPs given in Fig. 3, the absorption band examined at 546.39 cm−1 confirming the presence of Fe–O group [60]. The SIN spectrum presented distinctive peaks at 1269 cm−1 (C-O), 1166 cm−1 (C-C-C) and 3455 cm−1 (-OH). SIN-MGNs spectrum displayed a broad peak at 3300 cm−1 and new peaks that are mentioned for SIN, SIN-mag-NPs and GQDs were present with smaller intensities, this showed that the drug was incorporated in the SIN-MGNs [61].

Fig. 4 presents the decreased saturation magnetization of SIN-MGNs (4.54 emu/g) as compared to SIN-mag-NPs (6.70 emu/g). This change showed the encapsulation of SIN-mag-NPs within the GQDs solution [4,61].

Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs and SIN-MGNs at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. As examined in Fig. 5 GQDs exhibited a sharp emission peak at 435 nm and the fluorescence intensity of the formulation decreased with reduced SIN-MGNs concentration in the solution. Hence, it was determined that there was a success in modifying GQDs on SIN-MGNs [23,61].

As presented in Fig. 6, it was shown that 36.05 ± 0.36% of SIN released till 24 h and drug release improved and the cumulative amount of SIN release over 2 days was 45.16 ± 0.5%, the SIN release profile exhibited a sustained release pattern. In PBS, PLGA could be biodegraded by hydrolysis of ester linkage between them. So the much slower release from SIN-MGNs after 48 h could be attributed to slow diffusion of drug from the NPs interior, by the degradation of PLGA [62].

As presented in Fig. 7, SIN-MGNs exhibited higher cytotoxicity as compared to GQDs, at all tested concentrations. The IC50 values of GQDs and SIN-MGNs on A-498 cells were < 10 ± 0.14 µg/mL, 6 ± 1.37 µg/mL respectively. The preferential cytotoxicity of SIN-MGNs on A-498 cells can be attributed to the specificity of SIN to aberrantly expressed molecular targets in kidney cancer cells. Several studies have shown anticancer activity of SIN on A-498 cells. Through either SIN inhibiting cellular growth via the up-regulation of insulin-like growth factor binding protein 3 (IGFBP-3), or by inhibiting EGFR (epidermal growth factor receptor) signal-induced migration and invasion of kidney cancer cells [14,63,64].

Fig. 8 represented the fluorescent images and graphical representation of fluorescence intensity corresponding to GQDs and SIN-MGNs. The results clearly show that MGNs showed enhanced SIN delivery compared with GQDs. Due to the smaller particle size of SIN-MGNs formulation showed higher cellular uptake. This confirmed the anticancer effect of internalized SIN-MGNs on A-498 cells [59].

The plasma levels of SIN over 48 h resulting a single i.v. injection of SIN and SIN-MGNs at a dose of 50 mg/kg in mice were shown in Fig. 9 and pharmacokinetic parameters are mentioned in Table 6. The mean plasma-drug concentration-time curve demonstrated a comparable area under the curve, i. e., AUC0-∞ = 108.89 for SIN-MGNs and AUC0-∞ = 44.09 for SIN, respectively. With respect to SIN-MGNs, the initial drug concentration noticed after 0.5 h was 7.3 ± 21.56 µg/mL which increased with a Cmax and Tmax of 8.24 ± 17.89 µg/mL and 1 h, furthermore decreasing to a final concentration of 0.75 ± 3.9 µg/mL after 48 h. The clearance of SIN-MGNs was 7.57 mL/h and of free SIN was 39.00 mL/h. Also, the mean residence time (MRT) of SIN-MGNs in the plasma was 32.86 h, 6.53 times that of free SIN. Overall, SIN-MGNs had the longer t1/2 and MRT, lower CL than the free drug in mice. The sustained release of SIN from MGNs in the blood confirmed that the bioavailability of SIN increased when it was loaded into SIN-MGNs [65–67].

The results of PS, ZP and % EE confirmed little changes during the storage stability study ( Table 7). Further, IVR studies of SIN-MGNs presented no substantial change, which indicated the stability of formulations.

In the present study, SIN-mag-NP formulation was prepared and optimised by applying CCD and further used for the preparation of SIN-MGNs. It has been found that SIN is effectively encapsulated (81.27 ± 0.98) in SIN-MGNs having PS 250.6 ± 0.61 nm. The release behaviour of SIN from SIN-MGNs displayed sustained delivery for 48 h. SIN-MGNs displayed higher cmax, longer MRT and 5.15 fold augmentation in drug bioavailability as compared to free SIN and also inhibited the growth of A-498 cells by modulating EGFR signal-induced migration and invasion of kidney cancer cells. Therefore, the magnetographene quantum dot nanocomposites considered as an interesting alternative for SIN delivery and promising treatment option for kidney cancer.
Anjali Takke: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Pravin Shende: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests.
